coredata.dc:title: Architecting 3D prism shaped carbon dots/germanium/germanium oxide nanohybrid for photocatalytic degradation of pendimethalin and dinotefuran pesticides
coredata.dc:creator: Dhenadhayalan, Namasivayam
Chauhan, Anuj
Lin, King-Chuen
AlFantazi, Akram
coredata.dc:description: A highly efficient photocatalyst ‘carbon dots-reinforced germanium/germanium oxide’ (CGGO) nanohybrid material was developed for an investigation of photocatalytic activity towards toxic insecticide [dinotefuran (DTF)] and herbicide [pendimethalin (PDM)]. The facile hydrothermal method was applied to synthesize CGGO with the presence of carbon dots (C-dots) as a reductant and shape tuner. A 3D-shaped nanoprism morphology of CGGO was acquired in which Ge/GeO2 was encapsulated by a carbon layer, whereas the nanosheets-like layer structure of reduced GeO2 (rGeO2) was observed in the absence of C-dots, revealing the shape tuning proficiency of C-dots. CGGO was exhibited superior photocatalytic activity under visible light illumination (>440 nm) as such ∼100% degradation of PDM and DTF was achieved in 22 and 14 min with a high rate constant of 0.1448 and 0.2503min−1, respectively. The degradation mechanism was predominantly governed by the hydroxyl radical reaction of CGGO with pesticides, which was confirmed based on the catalytic reactions with reactive species scavengers. In addition, the photogenerated electrons were trapped by the carbon layer, which in turn suppresses the recombination of photogenerated electron and hole pairs leading to enhanced photocatalytic activity. The synergistic effects of Ge/GeO2 and C-dots make the CGGO nanohybrid a superior photocatalyst. Furthermore, reusable and practical applicability in real sample analyses were performed to validate the sustainability of nanohybrid.

head.ce:title: Architecting 3D prism shaped carbon dots/germanium/germanium oxide nanohybrid for photocatalytic degradation of pendimethalin and dinotefuran pesticides

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A highly efficient photocatalyst ‘carbon dots-reinforced germanium/germanium oxide’ (CGGO) nanohybrid material was developed for an investigation of photocatalytic activity towards toxic insecticide [dinotefuran (DTF)] and herbicide [pendimethalin (PDM)]. The facile hydrothermal method was applied to synthesize CGGO with the presence of carbon dots (C-dots) as a reductant and shape tuner. A 3D-shaped nanoprism morphology of CGGO was acquired in which Ge/GeO2 was encapsulated by a carbon layer, whereas the nanosheets-like layer structure of reduced GeO2 (rGeO2) was observed in the absence of C-dots, revealing the shape tuning proficiency of C-dots. CGGO was exhibited superior photocatalytic activity under visible light illumination (>440 nm) as such ∼100% degradation of PDM and DTF was achieved in 22 and 14 min with a high rate constant of 0.1448 and 0.2503min−1, respectively. The degradation mechanism was predominantly governed by the hydroxyl radical reaction of CGGO with pesticides, which was confirmed based on the catalytic reactions with reactive species scavengers. In addition, the photogenerated electrons were trapped by the carbon layer, which in turn suppresses the recombination of photogenerated electron and hole pairs leading to enhanced photocatalytic activity. The synergistic effects of Ge/GeO2 and C-dots make the CGGO nanohybrid a superior photocatalyst. Furthermore, reusable and practical applicability in real sample analyses were performed to validate the sustainability of nanohybrid.

ce:section.ce:para: Among several kinds of pollutants, pesticides are renowned to contain much stronger toxic compounds, causing severe damage to environments and human health [1–4]. Pesticides have an advantage in enhancing food production by controlling pests, insects, and weeds. To achieve high yield production, pesticides are used in agricultural fields with approved regulation levels. However, high-level usage of pesticides might certainly contaminate foods and agricultural land. Pesticides exist in drinking water, potentially causing health effects even at a trace level of contamination [4]. For instance, carbamates and organophosphates-based pesticides would disturb the nervous system, while numerous pesticides are carcinogenic inherently [5–9]. Among pesticides, pendimethalin (PDM) and dinotefuran (DTF) are widely used to protect diverse crops including rice, cereals, vegetables, and fruits from different kinds of weeds and insects [10–14]. PDM is a dinitroaniline-based herbicide and DTF is one of the neonicotinoid-based insecticides; both pesticides are considerably soluble in water, so they can be certainly persisted in the pesticides-treated agricultural soil, or conversely, they possibly contaminate into surface water. Due to such negative impacts of pesticides, the degradation of pesticides is an important task to validate food and water quality as well as environmental systems. Photocatalysis is a well-known promising method for transforming toxic pollutants into harmless products [15–19]. Solar energy utilizes this method to activate the catalytic reaction for fixing these kinds of environmental issues.

Semiconducting materials are broadly used as photocatalysts due to their unique features including quantum confinement effect with longer wavelength absorption, excellent charge carrier, etc. [16,18,20–22]. The photogenerated electron-hole (
e −
−
h +
) pair formation due to photon absorption of the semiconductor plays an important role in the photocatalytic reaction. How to prevent feasible recombination of
e −
−
h +
pair is a vital task for developing an efficient photocatalyst. Thus, the photocatalyst materials must possess the ability to suppress the recombination of
e −
−
h +
pairs for enhancing their catalytic activity. From this perspective, germanium oxide (GeO2) possesses the advantages of a strong broad absorption band in the UV–visible range with a variable bandgap, thereby extensively used in optical devices-based applications [23–26]. In addition, the presence of defect sites such as oxygen vacancies in GeO2 serves as carrier trapping sites, which prolong the separation period between e− and h+ pairs. In general, bulk GeO2 has a bandgap energy of ∼5–6 eV whereas nanoparticle-based GeO2 exhibits ∼2.5–4 eV bandgap with emission in the violet to blue wavelength region as controlled by the oxygen vacancies on the surface [24,27,28]. Their optical properties and structural stability could be further enhanced through doping or encapsulation with external compounds to achieve desired applications. For instance, carbon-based materials are used to coat Ge/GeO2 nanoparticles for controlling their particle size and surface properties [29,30]. By taking advantage of graphene and graphene oxide, the graphene/GeO2 nanocomposite was developed for electrochemistry-related applications especially for lithium-ion batteries because the excellent electronic conductivity of graphene enhances electron transport and cycling stability [31–33]. Zhao et al. have developed Ge/mesoporous carbon composite for high-performance lithium-ion batteries, in which the material showed stable electrochemical performance with excellent electrical conductivity [34]. Besides, many carbon matrices were applied to design novel nanocomposites with features of core-shell structure and porous structure for preventing Ge particles from aggregation, increasing surface area, and good stability [29,34–36]. Apart from the electrochemical applications, Ge/GeO2 materials have been much less utilized in the catalysis application [37–39].

Similar to Ge/GeO2, carbon-dots (C-dots) have several unique features and are extensively utilized as a superior probe in sensor and catalysis applications [40–42]. Their physicochemical properties can easily be regulated by doping of elements or modification of surface functional groups [40]. Moreover, the C-dots allow chemically interacting with diverse kinds of materials to form a nanohybrid. Recently, we have fabricated core-shell-based nanohybrid of C-dots-encapsulated metal nanoparticles (Ru and Pd) in which the C-dots served as a reductant and capping agent [43,44]. These nanohybrids were demonstrated excellent catalytic activity towards diverse pollutants; the C-dots layer may enhance the catalytic efficiency through the interfacial region. By taking advantage of Ge/GeO2 and C-dots, the present work intends to develop a functional nanohybrid as a photocatalyst.

Herein, the C-dots-supported Ge/GeO2 (CGGO) nanohybrid was synthesized by using the hydrothermal method. In general, a variety of biowastes could be facilely transformed into the formation of C-dots [40,41]. In this work, the C-dots were synthesized from the biowaste of papaya seeds, and the prepared C-dots were used as a reducing agent and stabilizer to prepare the CGGO nanohybrid. The as-prepared CGGO was manifested as a photocatalyst in the degradation of PDM and DTF pesticides upon irradiation of visible light. A UV–visible absorption spectrophotometer was employed to monitor the extent of degradation. The catalytic efficiencies were determined, and an appreciable mechanism was proposed. Furthermore, the stability and practical applicability of CCGO were investigated.
The details about materials and instrumentations adopted are given in supporting information.

C-dots were prepared from papaya seeds by using the pyrolysis method (Scheme 1 a). A certain amount of washed papaya seeds was heated in a microwave oven (∼500 W, 10 min) followed by crushing to make a powder. Then, the powder (∼1.0 g) was heated at 400 °C for 3 h with a ramp of 2 °Cmin−1 in a temperature-programmed electrical tube furnace under a nitrogen atmosphere. The resultant product was dissolved in distilled water and sonicated for 15 min. The obtained brown-colored solution was centrifuged (11,000 rpm, 15 min) to remove the bulk particles and impurities followed by dialysis of the supernatant using a membrane against distilled water for 24 h to acquire pure papaya seeds-derived C-dots.

CGGO was prepared by using the hydrothermal method as shown in Scheme 1b. Initially, the C-dots solution (5 mL, 5 μg/mL) was mixed with the aqueous solution of GeO2 (500 mg, 5 mL). The solution was heated at 70 °C for 20 h with continuous stirring under nitrogen flow conditions. Then, the resultant solution was purified by centrifugation and filtration with a membrane filter to obtain a pure product.

For the preparation of reduced GeO2 (rGeO2) in the absence of C-dots, the GeO2 was reduced by a simple reduction method. Briefly, the ammonium hydroxide (NH4OH, 3 mL) was added to the aqueous solution of GeO2 (0.5 g, 20 mL). The mixture was heated at 70 °C with continuous stirring under a nitrogen atmosphere. After heating for 10 min, sodium borohydride (NaBH4, 0.5 M, 2 mL) was rapidly added into the mixture followed by heating continuously for a further 20 h. It was noticed that the solution color was immediately turned to brown color after the addition of NaBH4. Finally, the mixture was washed well with distilled water by centrifugation and then dried the product to obtain pure rGeO2.

Tauc plot was used to estimate the bandgap energy of materials using the following Kubelka−Munk [F(R)] relation [45]:
F
( R )
h
ν
1 n
= C
(
h ν −
E g
)
where E
g is the bandgap energy, h is the Planck constant, ν is the photon frequency, C is a constant. The factor n is equal to ½ or 2 corresponding to the direct and indirect transition bandgap, respectively. The term F(R)hν was calculated using reflectance spectral values obtained from the UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy measurements. The resultant (F(R)hν)2 values were plotted against photon energy, exhibiting a steep and linearly increasing curve with increasing photon energy. The bandgap energy was estimated by the x-axis intersection of the linear region of the Tauc plot.

An aqueous solution of PDM (0.1 mM) and DTF (0.15 mM) was individually prepared and used as a stock solution. For the photocatalytic reaction of PDM, a certain concentration of CGGO solution was added into the fixed volume of the PDM solution. Then, the mixture was stirred under dark conditions for 30 min. After that, the mixture was irradiated by visible light (xenon) at a power of 1.2 W with continuous stirring. To eliminate UV radiation, the UV cut-off filter was placed in-between the light source and sample holder, allowing the transmission of visible light above 440 nm (Fig. S1). The distance between the light source and sample holder was fixed to be 15 cm. During the light illumination, the UV–visible absorption spectrum of sample solution was monitored at regular periods. Then, different concentrations of CGGO were applied to perform the photocatalytic degradation of PDM. Similarly, the photocatalytic degradation of DTF was investigated by varying the concentrations of CGGO. Finally, the rate constants were calculated for each catalytic reaction.

A similar procedure was followed to verify the practical applicability of CGGO. Briefly, various vegetables were soaked in distilled water for 2 h and the obtained solution was used as a polluted water source. Then, the vegetable-soaked wastewater solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min. Finally, a certain concentration of DTF and CGGO was added into the obtained supernatant followed by irradiation under visible light. Similarly, photocatalytic degradation of DTF was performed in pond water. The pond water was collected from our university campus, and the water solution was filtered with a filter membrane (0.22 μm, Millipore) followed by centrifugation (11,000 rpm, 30 min) to remove solid impurities.

To determine the reusability of the CGGO catalyst, the sample solution was ultra-centrifuged after the first catalytic reaction. The residue was washed with distilled water and ethanol to obtain a pure catalyst for the further sequential catalytic reaction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of rate constant and efficiency of degradation
ce:section.ce:para:
The photo-degradation efficiencies for both catalytic reactions of PDM and DTF were determined by using the following equation [46]:
Degradation
( % )
=
C 0
−
C t
C 0
× 100
where C
0 and C
t are pesticide concentrations at initial (t = 0) and certain time (t), respectively. The reaction rate constant (k) was evaluated by pseudo-first-order rate kinetics equation as follows:
ln
(
C t
C 0
)
= − k t

The calculated values of ln (C
t /C
0 ) were plotted against reaction time to obtain a linear relationship. The slope value of the linear plot provides the reaction rate constant.

The structural morphology of as-prepared C-dots, rGeO2, and CGGO was examined by recording field emission transmission electron microscope (FETEM) images. The resultant FETEM image of C-dots displays spherical-shaped particles with an average of ∼5 nm (Fig. 1 a), revealing the successful formation of C-dots from the papaya seeds. The rGeO2 without C-dots exhibits a nanosheet-like layer structure as shown in Fig. 1b and c. Interestingly, the CGGO was formed as trigonal bipyramidal-like structures (nanoprism) as shown in Fig. 1d–f. The different views (top, side, and bottom) of 3D-shaped nanoprism are seen in Fig. 1d and e. The energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) was further examined to validate the chemical composition of CGGO and the color-mapping images of each element are compiled in Fig. 1g. The resultant EDX color mapping exhibits the presence of Ge and O atoms, which confirms the formation of CGGO with nanoprism morphology. This finding indicates that C-dots played a role as a shape tuner to transform the sheet-like morphology of GeO2 into a nanoprism shape. It was previously reported that the C-dots could function as a reducing agent and its spherical morphology was transformed as a carbon layer coated on metal nanoparticles [43,44]. Herein, two different phases were apparently seen in the higher magnitude FETEM image (Fig. 1f), and the appearance of C and O (exhibiting high intensity in a large area compared to Ge) in EDX color-mapping images (Fig. 1g) implies that the carbon layer was formed over Ge/GeO2 nanohybrid. However, the carbon layer morphology and the boundary between the Ge/GeO2 and carbon layer could not be clearly distinguished due to several effects including the disordered nature of carbon structure, deformation of GeO2, and the carbon atoms being incorporated into interstitial sites of GeO2 during the heating process [47,48].

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis was investigated to determine the crystal structure of rGeO2 and CGGO nanohybrid as shown in Fig. 2 . The XRD pattern of rGeO2 shows significant sharp peaks along with slight broadening (Fig. 2a), which implies that rGeO2 presents in the form of both crystalline and amorphous. The peaks observed at 20.8, 26.3, 36.5, 58.4, and 62.8° correspond to the (100), (101), (110), (121), and (113) planes of hexagonal phase GeO2 which are perfectly matched with diffraction peaks obtained for raw GeO2 (Fig. 2b) [27,39,49]. Further, the weak peaks at 27.6 and 53.3° index to the (110) and (211) planes of cubic phase Ge [39]. This result confirms the presence of two crystalline phases in rGeO2, and the appearance of Ge due to the reduction of GeO2. For the case of CGGO (Fig. 2a), the XRD pattern exhibits similar sharp peaks at the same positions as obtained in rGeO2, signifying the existence of two crystalline phases corresponding to Ge and GeO2. However, the intensity of peak at 27.4° [(110) plane of Ge] was found to significantly increase as compared to that of rGeO2, indicating the efficient reduction of GeO2 by the C-dots. Furthermore, the observed strong broad diffraction peaks at ∼22.5 and 45° are ascribed to the amorphous characteristic of C-dots [44].

The structural features of CGGO were further characterized by Raman spectroscopy. The Raman spectrum of raw GeO2 shows multiple peaks representing the optical modes of the GeO2 and Ge phases, in which the modes of GeO2 are more dominant than those of Ge (Fig. 3 a). The strong peak at ∼437 cm−1 and weak peaks at ∼260, 340, 600, 711, and 765 cm−1 are characteristics of Raman active modes of GeO2 in which the observed peak at 437 cm−1 confirms the existence of hexagonal phase of GeO2 [39,50]. On the contrary, the intensity of peak at 437 cm−1 was significantly reduced in rGeO2 as shown in Fig. 3b. In addition, a new peak at ∼295 cm−1 with weak intensity represents a typical Raman mode of crystalline Ge formation, which is in good agreement with XRD results [39,50]. Interestingly, the peak of crystalline Ge (∼295 cm−1) was showed higher intensity than other peaks of GeO2 in the Raman spectrum of CGGO (Fig. 3b), signifying the predominant formation of Ge.

To explore the chemical composition and oxidation state, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was implemented for as-prepared C-dots, rGeO2, and CGGO. The obtained XPS spectra of C-dots were deconvoluted as shown in Fig. S2. The deconvoluted C1s spectrum displays four peaks at 284.1, 285.2, 286.1, and 288.5 eV corresponding to C=C, C–C, C–O, and C=O components, respectively (Fig. S2a). The observed C=C (sp2) and C–C (sp3) peaks are characteristic of the amorphous nature of the carbon core. Likewise, the C–O and C=O peaks infer the presence of carboxylic functional group which is further confirmed by the two deconvoluted peaks of O1s containing C=O (531 eV) and C–O (531.8 eV) (Fig. S2b) [44]. The deconvoluted XPS spectrum of N exhibits a single peak at 399 eV corresponds to the pyridinic-N (Fig. S2c), signifying the formation of N-doped C-dots [44]. The XPS spectra of Ge were deconvoluted for raw GeO2, rGeO2, and CGGO as shown in Fig. 4 . The XPS spectrum of Ge 3d for raw GeO2 exhibits a strong peak in the region of 29–36 eV that was deconvoluted into three peaks with a maximum of 34.2, 32.5, and 30.5 eV (Fig. 4a). Among them, the peaks at 34.2 and 32.5 eV are more dominant corresponding to the characteristic Ge 3d peaks of GeO2 and GeO, respectively, indicating the presence of Ge4+ and Ge2+ [24,34,51]. Also, the minor peak at 30.5 eV corresponds to Ge0 [51]. The deconvoluted XPS spectrum of Ge for rGeO2 shows three peaks at 34.1, 32.0, and 29.8 eV as similar to raw GeO2 (Fig. 4b). However, it can be seen that the contribution of GeO (32.0 eV) and Ge (29.8 eV) elements are considerably enhanced over GeO2 due to the reduction of GeO2. Interestingly, compared to the results of rGeO2, the deconvoluted XPS spectrum of Ge for CGGO exhibits more dominated peaks of Ge and GeO elements (Fig. 4c). The strong peak of Ge is further strengthened to the efficient reduction process in the presence of C-dots. The atomic percentage of Ge0 was determined using the integrated area of Ge peak and atomic sensitivity factors. The atomic percentage of Ge0 was estimated to be 18.7, 28.3, and 41.6% for GeO2, rGeO2, and CGGO, respectively. It implies that more amount of Ge0 is present in CGGO nanohybrid as compared to that of GeO2 and rGeO2. These obtained XPS results demonstrate that the C-dots might govern the reduction process of GeO2 to form Ge nanohybrid along with the nanoprism morphology of CGGO. In addition, the atomic percentages of Ge, O, C, and N elements in CGGO were estimated to be 30.6, 55.9, 12.4, and 1.1%, and corresponding weight percentages of 67.7, 27.3, 4.5, and 0.5%, respectively.

The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherm measurement was performed to determine the surface area and porosity nature of CGGO. The resultant N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms and pore size distributions plots are shown in Fig. S3. The Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) surface area of CGGO was estimated to be 28.64 m2/g which is higher than that of GeO2 (0.40 m2/g). In addition, Barrett–Joyner–Halenda (BJH) method was utilized to analyze the pore size distribution. The pore volume from the desorption curve and average pore size of CGGO were evaluated to be 0.1736 cm3/g and 12.04 nm, respectively, which are higher than those of GeO2 (pore volume 0.0014 cm3/g and average pore size 7.14 nm). Such higher surface area and pore volume/size in CGGO arise due to the formation of the carbon layer around Ge/GeO2 and the presence of defect sites. In general, the large pore volume and surface area of material provide more binding sites for the adsorption of pollutants on the surface, which facilitates an efficient photodegradation process. It is anticipated that the CGGO serves as an excellent photocatalyst due to its large surface area and pore volume.

During the hydrothermal process of the sample mixture, C-dots promote the efficient reduction of GeO2 resulting in the formation of Ge. C-dots are capable of serving as a reducing and capping agent. The as-prepared C-dots have hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups that facilitate electrostatic interaction with Ge leading to adsorption of C-dots on the surface of Ge/GeO2. The formed Ge atoms tend to agglomerate producing Ge nuclei. During the growth process, the Ge nuclei could be fused to form nanoprisms. The adsorbed C-dots might govern the formation of nanoprism shape by controlling the relative growth rates of specific facets and stabilizing the formed Ge nuclei. For instance, the C-dots core is composed of aromatic rings that may prefer to bind on Ge/GeO2 (101 and 111) surfaces due to the hexagonal array of atoms instead of the (100) facet. Previously, Chiu et al. have synthesized shape-controlled platinum nanocrystals by using facet-specific peptide sequences as a capping agent in which peptide S7 has the phenyl groups (–C6H5) that prefer to bind (111) surface [52]. Thus, the selective adsorption of C-dots on the (101 and 111) facets of Ge nuclei may lead to preferential growth of prism shape rather than cubic shape. It was previously reported that GeO2 prefers to form nanocubes due to its crystal facets. As stated by Wu et al., the particle shape of hexagonal GeO2 is composed of a hexagonal prism (100) in the middle with two hexagonal pyramids (101) at the ends [53]. During the growth process, the GeO2 crystal was changed to a cube-like shape [(011) and (101–)] from hexagonal bipyramidal [53,54]. However, the crystal shape of GeO2 could be modified by passivation with capping agents due to selective adsorption on specific facets of GeO2 [55,56].

However, nanoprism formation depends on the concentration of C-dots. To validate the involvement of C-dots as a shape tuner, the CGGO was prepared by varying concentrations of C-dots (0.1 and 2.5 μg/mL) using a similar synthesis procedure. The CGGO was denoted as CGGO-1 and CGGO-2 for the addition of low (0.1 μg/mL) and moderate (2.5 μg/mL) concentrations of C-dots, respectively. The FETEM images were recorded for CGGO-1 and CGGO-2 as shown in Fig. 5 . The resultant FETEM image of CGGO-1 shows nanosheet-like morphology (Fig. 5a) which is significantly different from the morphology of rGeO2 (without C-dots) (Fig. 1c). It was found in Fig. 5a that few nanoprisms were started to form at this condition. For the case of CGGO-2, the partially formed nanoprism-shaped CGGO was observed (Fig. 5b). The reduction process is inefficient in CGGO-1 and CGGO-2 due to low/moderate concentration of C-dots. Thus, the formation of Ge atoms and subsequent cluster formation is limited to making a nanoprism. That's why fewer nanoprisms along with irregular-shaped CGGO were produced in CGGO-1 and CGGO-2. When a high concentration of C-dots was added, the reduction process efficiently occurred that increasing the probability of full-grown nanoprism-shaped CGGO. From these findings, it can be concluded that the C-dots are mainly responsible for the obtained nanoprism-shaped CGGO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocurrent studies and determination of bandgap energy
ce:section.ce:para:
To examine the separation and recombination efficiency of photogenerated electrons and holes, the transient photocurrent measurement was performed for rGeO2 and CGGO at 0.9 V applied potential with 40 s switch-off and switch-on cycles. The resultant photocurrent responses are shown in Fig. 6 a. The CGGO nanohybrid displays a higher photocurrent response compared to that of rGeO2, which is due to the effective separation of photogenerated
e −
−
h +
pairs. It is anticipated that the carbon layer in CGGO functions as an excited electron trap resulting in the suppression of electron and hole recombination. Furthermore, the stability of CGGO was examined at an applied potential of 0.9 V for 800 s. The photocurrent response remains constant even at a longer time (Fig. 6b), signifying its higher stability nature for long-term cycles.

The diffuse reflectance spectrum of rGeO2 and CGGO was measured to determine the bandgap energy. The obtained reflectance values were applied in the Kubleka-Monk function of the Tauc plot [45]. The resultant Tauc plots are shown in Fig. 6c; the value of bandgap energy was determined by the x-axis intersection point of the linear fit. The bandgap energy of rGeO2 was found to be 2.26 eV whereas the bandgap was shifted to lower energy (1.95 eV) for CGGO. The lower bandgap energy of CGGO signifies a suitable material for visible light-based photocatalytic reactions. On the other hand, commercial pure GeO2 exhibits bandgap energy of 4.99 eV (Fig. S4) which is much higher than that of rGeO2 and CGGO. Compared to rGeO2, the decrease in bandgap energy in CGGO may be due to the encapsulation of the carbon layer over Ge/GeO2 that enhances its advantage as a desirable photocatalyst material. The carbon layer contains nitrogen in its core moiety along with hydroxyl and carboxylic surface functional groups. The lone-pair electrons in the nitrogen and oxygen elements might be delocalized in the system leading to the formation of localized states. Due to the increasing concentration of electrons in the system, the separation distance between electron-hole pairs becomes smaller and thereby bandgap will be decreased which is similar to the phenomenon observed in chemical-doping semiconductor materials. The observed lower bandgap energy of CGGO as compared to rGeO2 is in good agreement with the following absorption and fluorescence spectral results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Steady-state absorption and fluorescence spectral studies
ce:section.ce:para: The steady-state absorption and fluorescence spectra of the materials were recorded, and the resultant spectra are shown in Fig. S5. The absorption spectrum of GeO2 and rGeO2 exhibits a peak at ∼270 nm in which the absorption tail with weak absorbance extends to the visible wavelength range in rGeO2 (Fig. S5a). On the other hand, CGGO possesses a broad absorption spectrum in the UV–visible range with two shoulders at ∼270 and 340 nm due to the contribution of GeO2 and C-dots, respectively. The absorption spectral changes in rGeO2 and CGGO compared to GeO2 are in good agreement with the bandgap observations. Then, fluorescence spectra are measured at different excitation wavelengths. As shown in Figs. S5b and c, the fluorescence peak maximum of GeO2 and rGeO2 was observed at 412 and 430 nm, respectively. The redshifted peak position of rGeO2 may arise due to considerably higher defect sites such as oxygen vacancies present in rGeO2 as compared to that in GeO2. Previously, Seal et al. have observed similar redshifted fluorescence behavior of GeO2 nanoparticles (428 nm) as compared to bulk GeO2 (∼415 nm) which is due to the presence of a greater number of oxygen vacancies in GeO2 nanoparticles [37]. As previously reported that the fluorescence in the blue-green wavelength region originates due to the Ge-GeO2 interfacial defects and defect sites such as oxygen/germanium vacancies [37,50,55]. Interestingly, the CGGO shows different fluorescence behavior such as two prominent peaks with a maximum of ∼435 and 480 nm while varying excitation wavelengths (Fig. S5d). When the excitation wavelength was tuned from 300 to 380 nm, a broad fluorescence peak with a maximum of ∼435 nm was observed along with a shoulder at ∼480 nm. It was found that the intensity of the shoulder increases as increasing excitation wavelengths. At the excitation of 400 nm, the prominent fluorescence peak was observed with a maximum of 480 nm which was further redshifted upon increasing the excitation wavelength above 400 nm. These observed longer wavelength fluorescence with excitation-dependent redshift behavior are well-known unique features of C-dots. Accordingly, fluorescence in the lower wavelength region is ascribed to the Ge/GeO2 domain, whereas longer wavelength fluorescence corresponds to the C-dots domain. Thus, it can be concluded that the fluorescence of CGGO originates from both Ge/GeO2 and C-dots domains.

To investigate the capability of CGGO as a photocatalyst, catalytic degradation measurements were conducted towards diverse kinds of toxic PDM (herbicide) and DTF (insecticide) pesticides by using the photocatalysis method. To determine the photocatalytic activity, an aqueous solution of the respective pesticide in the presence of CGGO was irradiated under continuous illumination of visible light. Subsequently, time-dependent absorbance changes due to catalytic reactions were monitored at regular periods by optical absorption measurement.

The photocatalytic degradation of PDM was evaluated in the absence and presence of different concentrations of CGGO. Fig. 7 demonstrates the obtained results for the degradation of PDM. The absorption spectrum of PDM in the absence of catalyst displays the peak maximum at ∼450 nm. The absorption spectral changes of PDM with CGGO were monitored after stirring the mixture under dark conditions (30 min), exhibiting no significant change in the peak absorbance. In contrast, the peak absorbance (∼450 nm) was found to gradually decrease under the illumination of visible light as shown in Fig. 7a–c. It was noticed that a new peak appeared at ∼380 nm and subsequently disappeared during the degradation process. The extent of degradation was dependent on the concentration of the CGGO. For instance, the complete degradation of PDM was finished in 60 min in the presence of a lower concentration of CGGO (5.0 μg/mL) (Fig. 7a) whereas the reaction rate increases with the increasing concentration of CGGO. The degradation process was found to occur much faster with a higher concentration of CGGO (60 μg/mL) such that the process was completed within 22 min (Fig. 7c), revealing the effective catalytic nature of CGGO. To verify the influence of catalyst, a similar degradation procedure was followed to illuminate PDM without the addition of CGGO. The observed absorption spectral changes demonstrate an insignificant degradation process as the <20% degradation efficiency was found even at a longer time (120 min) (Fig. 7d and e). This finding further strengthens the efficacy of CGGO toward the degradation of PDM.

As similar to PDM, the photocatalytic degradation of DTF was scrutinized by monitoring absorption spectral changes in the presence of CGGO catalyst, and the resultant spectral results are shown in Fig. 8 . The absorption spectrum of DTF displays a strong peak at ∼270 nm. Under dark conditions, the absorbance of this peak with CGGO was not altered even after 30 min stirring, indicating an inefficient catalytic reaction of DTF without light. While illumination with visible light, the extent of photocatalytic degradation of DTF was observed until the absorption peak (270 nm) was completely diminished, as shown in Fig. 8a–c. When the concentration of CGGO varied from low to high, the photocatalytic reaction becomes more feasible as the nearly 100% degradation process occurs within a shorter time (14 min) whereas the degradation process is insignificant in the absence of CGGO even for a longer time (2 h) light illumination (Fig. 8d).

By following pseudo-first-order rate kinetics, the logarithmic absorbance changes as a function of time were plotted for each concentration of CGGO (Fig. 8e). The rate constant value was obtained from the slope value of the linear plot. The catalytic reaction with a higher concentration of CGGO (60 μg/mL) yields a much higher rate constant of 0.2503min−1, which evidences the superior performance of CGGO as a photocatalyst. In addition, the determined rate constant is much higher than previously reported values (Table 2) [61,62].

As similar to CGGO, the photocatalytic activity of rGeO2 was investigated towards the degradation of PDM and DTF. For the comparison of catalytic performance between CGGO and rGeO2, a similar experimental procedure was followed with a high concentration of rGeO2 (60 μg/mL). Fig. 9 displays the obtained degradation results of PDM and DTF. The absorption peak of PDM/DTF was found to diminish upon light illumination, revealing the good catalytic efficacy of rGeO2. However, it was found that the degradation rate was slower than that observed with CGGO. For instance, the degradation process of PDM and DTF was completed in 40 min (93%) and 35 min (95%) with yielding rate constants of 0.0701 and 0.0928min−1, respectively. When comparing rate constants, the CGGO manifests a higher rate constant than that of rGeO2 which validates the superior photocatalytic performance of CGGO.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mineralization studies of PDM and DFT
ce:section.ce:para: Total organic carbon (TOC) analysis measurement was performed to understand the mineralization of PDM and DTF. The mineralization of PDM/DTF was evaluated in the presence of CGGO (60 μg/mL). The resultant TOC removal efficiencies are shown in Fig. S6. In the initial degradation process, 16 and 44% of mineralization were acquired for PDM and DTF at 5 min irradiation. Then, the efficiency of TOC removal reached ∼77 and 84% after 25- and 20-min irradiation for PDM and DTF, respectively, indicating both pesticides were mineralized to great extent. This finding reveals that the CGGO shows a significant effect on the mineralization of both PDM and DTF into residual organic species.

Upon illumination of CGGO, the electrons are excited to the conduction band (CB) from the valence band (VB) generating electrons (e–) and holes (h+) in the CB and VB, respectively (Scheme 2 ). It is well known that the efficacy of photogenerated
e −
−
h +
pairs depend on the absorption capability of the catalyst. Such
e −
−
h +
pairs can be effectively produced if the catalyst has strong absorbance in the visible region while illuminating visible light. The CGGO exhibits strong absorbance in the visible region, which is in good agreement with obtained bandgap energy in the lower range (1.95 eV). The photogenerated electrons and holes can react with dissolved oxygen and water molecules to yield superoxide radical anion (
O 2
. −
) and hydroxyl radical (
O
H .
), respectively [16,46,60]. These two species
O 2
. −
and
O
H .
would efficiently involve in the degradation process by reduction and oxidation reaction, respectively. The formation efficacy of
O 2
. −
and
O
H .
species depends on the separation of
e −
−
h +
pairs. As such, inhibition of
e −
−
h +
pairs recombination may enhance the production of reactive species, thereby achieving higher photocatalytic activity.

To confirm the involvement of reactive species in the degradation process, the photocatalytic degradation of DTF with CGGO was investigated in the presence of scavengers. Here, ammonium oxalate (AO), isopropyl alcohol (IPA), and benzoquinone (BQ) were individually used as a scavenger of hole,
O
H .
, and
O 2
. −
species, respectively [63]. A certain fixed concentration (0.2 mM) of AO, IPA, and BQ was individually added into the DTF sample solution containing CGGO (60 μg/mL), then the degradation of DTF was monitored and the obtained results are shown in Fig. S7. The efficiency of DTF degradation was estimated for each scavenger measurement with respect to reaction without scavenger. The degradation efficiency was significantly lowered to 14% in the presence of IPA while ∼65 and 85% efficiencies were acquired with AO and BQ scavenger, respectively. This finding implies the dominant role of
O
H .
as a reactive species for degradation as compared to hole and
O 2
. −
[59,64]. The degradation rate was considerably decreased due to the
O 2
. −
species scavenged by the addition of BQ, suggesting that there is a significant separation between photoexcited
e −
−
h +
pairs. Thereby, the holes could feasibly transform into
O
H .
which was then efficiently involved in the degradation process. However, there is a competitive reaction between the reactive species during the degradation process.

The positions of the CB and VB potentials were calculated using the bandgap energy and the resultant energy diagram is shown in Fig. S8. The values of ECB and EVB of CGGO were estimated to be 0.90 and 2.85 eV, respectively. The calculated VB potential (2.85 EV) is more positive than the standard redox potential of H2O/OH·(2.40 eV vs. NHE) [65], implying that the photogenerated holes in the VB of CGGO could react with water molecules to produce hydroxyl radical species. Thus, formed hydroxyl radicals with strong oxidizability were feasibly predominated in the degradation reaction of pesticides. On the other hand, the CB potential of CGGO (0.90 eV) is more positive than the standard redox potential of
O 2
/
O 2
⋅ −
(−0.33 eV vs. NHE) [65], indicating that the photogenerated electrons in the CB are not able to reduce O2 into superoxide species. However, the stored electrons in the CB can be transferred to the carbon layer due to the interfacial contact between Ge/GeO2 and the carbon layer. Such a trapping process of excited electrons favors prolonging the photogenerated
e −
−
h +
pair's recombination. This effect facilitates the generation of plenty of holes and subsequent more
O
H .
species production to involve in the catalytic process. In addition, it is presumed that the accumulated electrons in the carbon layer may react with O2 to produce superoxide followed by involvement in the degradation process [65,66]. In addition to the involvement of
O
H .
and
O 2
. −
reactive species, the light radiation will also contribute to photocatalytic degradation through direct photolysis.

The fluorescence spectral technique was utilized to further verify the formation
O
H .
radicals on the CGGO surface using terephthalic acid (TA) as a probe molecule. The non-fluorescent nature of TA could emit fluorescence in the form of hydroxyterephthalic acid (HTA) due to the reaction between TA and
O
H .
radicals [67]. As shown in Fig. S9, the fluorescence spectrum of TA (10 μM) with CGGO was measured at 320 nm excitation; the resulting spectrum displays no peaks in the range of 350–600 nm. When the solution was irradiated by visible light, a new peak appeared at ∼430 nm and its intensity gradually increased with the increasing irradiation time. This new peak is ascribed to the formation of HTA, confirming the effective generation of
O
H .
radicals on the surface of CGGO upon light illumination.

Based on the observed absorption spectral results, it is assumed that the photocatalytic degradation of PDM is initiated with the cleavage of alkyl groups in the secondary amine. It was confirmed by the observed decrease in absorbance at ∼445 nm which is due to n→π∗ transition [68]. In addition, the new peak appeared at a shorter wavelength (∼386 nm) with fading of the yellow-colored solution, indicating the formation of intermediate due to the conversion of a nitro group into amine group, and subsequent degradation into final products of CO2 and H2O molecules [60,69]. Similarly, according to previous reports, the plausible pathway of DTF degradation proceeds through cleavage of N–N bond followed by the removal of a nitro group, which is responsible for the initial decreasing absorbance at 269 nm. It is anticipated that the hydroxyl radical could interact with the C=N bond to form the C=O bond which is then separated from the five-membered ring leading to the final products (CO2 and H2O) [13,61].

Furthermore, mass spectrometric measurement was conducted to identify the formation of intermediates during the degradation reaction. According to obtained results of the mass spectrum (Figs. S10 and S11), the degradation pathways of DTF and PDM were identified as shown in Scheme 2. In the case of DTF, two possible pathways might involve in the photocatalytic process through oxidative reactions. Pathway 1 is mainly governed by hydroxylation, the hydroxyl radicals interact with DTF where the hydroxylation arises on the branched-chain of furan ring leading to the intermediate DTF-1 (m/z 235) (Fig. S10). Subsequent oxidation causes breaking of branched-chain results in the formation of intermediate DTF-2 which was confirmed by the observed fragment peak at 103 m/z (Fig. S10d) [14]. In pathway 2, the photocatalytic process was initiated with the elimination of nitro group resulting in the formation of DTF-3 (m/z 158) [14,70]. Subsequently, the DTF-3 was degraded by the elimination of secondary amine groups due to the interaction of hydroxyl radical with the C=N bond to generate C=O bond in DTF-4 (m/z 158) followed by cleavage of NH–CO leads to the formation of DTF-5 (m/z 104), which were confirmed by the respective mass peaks (Figs. S10c and d). Similarly, the degradation pathway of PDM was validated from the results of the mass spectrum (Fig. S11). As previously reported that three possible pathways were involved in the degradation of PDM [68]. Here, in accordance with results of absorption and mass spectral changes, the PDM was mainly degraded through the intermediate formation of 2-(hydroxyamino)-5,6-dimethyl-3-nitrophenol (PDM-4) (Scheme 2), which was supported by the molecular ion m/z 199 (Fig. S11c). Initially, the hydroxyl radicals oxidize the branched alkyl chain on the aromatic ring, subsequently hydroxylation in the benzene ring. Then, PDM-4 undergoes further degradation leading to the 2,3-dimethylphenol (PDM-6, m/z 123) (Figs. S11c and d) followed by complete degradation of PDM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability and practical applicability of CGGO
ce:section.ce:para: The reusability nature of CGGO was evaluated by consecutive photocatalytic degradation of PDM. The catalyst was recovered after the first recycling catalytic process by centrifugation to obtain residue followed by a wash with ethanol and dried. Then, a recovered catalyst was used for further recycling experiments. Finally, the catalytic efficiency was determined by comparing the obtained rate constants at each recycling process with the first-cycled rate constant. The resultant efficiency shows ∼96% at the second recycling process which further reduces to ∼74% after completing the sixth cycle (Fig. 10 ). The observed result signifies the excellent stability of CGGO. Furthermore, the FETEM image of recycled CGGO was recorded to verify the changes in the morphology (Fig. S12). The obtained FETEM image shows most of the species remained in the same morphology as compared to newly prepared CGGO. But few species display fading of nanoprism morphology due to the leaching effect.

The vegetables-soaked wastewater and pond water were taken as polluted sources to investigate the practical applicability of CGGO. A certain concentration of CGGO (60 μg/mL) was added into the DTF-added wastewater solution followed by irradiation of visible light. At regular periods, the absorption spectra of polluted wastewater were monitored, showing gradual absorbance changes with time during the degradation of DTF (Fig. S13). It was found that DTF was completely degraded within a shorter time. The rate constants were determined to be 0.2320 and 0.2407min−1 for the degradation of DTF in vegetable-soaked and pond wastewater, respectively. These results validate the photocatalytic activity of CGGO in real samples.

CGGO was showed superior photocatalytic activity towards PDM and DTF, capable of achieving a faster degradation rate. In general, a photocatalyst should have unique features including (1) semiconductor-based material, (2) ability to activate under UV/visible light illumination, and (3) efficient generation of
e −
−
h +
pairs with prevention of recombination. As such, the CGGO fulfills the photocatalyst requirements and possesses several advantages. For instance, the simple hydrothermal method was implemented to synthesize CGGO, in which the non-toxic nature of C-dots served as a reductant and shape tuner. The oxygen vacancies in GeO2 and the formation of carbon layer around Ge/GeO2 might be advantageous for the adsorption of pollutants on the surface of CGGO to enhance the catalytic activity. The CGGO exhibits a wide range of absorption wavelengths which facilitates the absorption of UV/visible light efficiently. The C-dots layer contains electron-withdrawing functional groups (-COOH) which prevent the recombination of
e −
−
h +
pairs by trapping the excited electrons following excitation of CGGO. The high catalytic efficacy can be achieved due to the prolonged separation of
e −
−
h +
pairs. Consequently, a diverse-natured mixture of organic (C-dots) and inorganic (GeO2)-based nanohybrid will opt for the photocatalysis application towards the degradation of different kinds of pollutants.

CGGO nanohybrid was synthesized by using a facile hydrothermal method. The pyrolysis method was applied to prepare C-dots from the papaya seeds. The morphology and structural properties of CGGO were well-characterized; the CGGO featured a nanoprism-shaped nanohybrid in which C-dots functioned as a reductant and shape tuner. The CGGO was implemented as a photocatalyst towards the degradation of PDM and DTF pesticides. The photocatalytic activity was examined with varying concentrations of CGGO by inspecting the absorption spectral changes with time. It was observed that the respective pesticides were completely degraded within a shorter period with high rate constants, revealing the excellent photocatalytic nature of CGGO. The plausible mechanism was proposed with the aid of scavengers, revealing that the hydroxyl radical is more dominant as reactive species involved in the degradation process. Thus, it leads to the conclusion that the CGGO can be synthesized by a simple procedure and can achieve highly efficient photocatalytic degradation towards toxic pollutants. Moreover, it is anticipated that the CGGO can be further chemically modified with external substances including metal nanoparticles and semiconducting materials to develop new emergent materials for various catalysis applications.

Namasivayam Dhenadhayalan: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing- Original draft. Anuj Chauhan: Investigation. King-Chuen Lin: Writing- Review & Editing, Supervision. Akram Alfantazi: Writing- Review & Editing, Supervision.
